text.title: clean-architecture Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the clean-architecture methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Source code dependencies must point only inward: Frameworks → Interface Adapters → Use Cases → Entities. Nothing in an inner circle may know anything about an outer circle.
rule.rationale: Inward-only dependencies make business rules stable and framework-independent.

rule.statement: Enforce the dependency rule with architecture tests (import-linter, ArchUnit). The rule only holds if it is machine-checked.
rule.rationale: Developers and LLMs routinely import ORM models into entities; without automated enforcement violations accumulate silently.

rule.statement: Entities (domain/): Enterprise business rules. No framework imports. Plain dataclasses or classes; framework-independent, DB-independent.
rule.rationale: An entity importing SQLAlchemy ties business rules to a specific ORM.

rule.statement: Use Cases (application/): Application-specific rules. Orchestrate entities via repository interfaces. Input DTOs / Output DTOs. No HTTP, no ORM.
rule.rationale: Use cases define WHAT, not HOW; HTTP/ORM details belong outside.

rule.statement: Interface Adapters (presentation/): Controllers, presenters, gateways translating between external formats and use-case DTOs. No business logic.
rule.rationale: Business logic in controllers cannot be reused across delivery mechanisms.

rule.statement: Frameworks and Drivers (infrastructure/): ORM impls, external APIs, message brokers. Implement interfaces defined in inner layers; never imported FROM inner layers.
rule.rationale: This is the inversion of control that makes the dependency rule work.

rule.statement: Skip when the input does not satisfy the Applies-If preconditions or another methodology owns this output shape.
rule.rationale: Prevents duplicate work and wrong-tool application.
